concept_name: Three Special Cases of Vector Addition — 0 deg, 90 deg, 180 deg
source_book: DC Pandey Chapter 5.2 — shortcuts for common angle cases; split from vector_addition bundle

real_world_anchor.primary: Three everyday moments the three special angles appear. Two kids pushing a swing from behind together — parallel forces, angle zero, effective push is the sum. A kite on Makar Sankranti — wind pushing horizontally, string pulling vertically, angle ninety, answer needs Pythagoras. A tug-of-war match on Republic Day at school — two teams pulling a rope from opposite ends, angle one hundred eighty, the winning force is the DIFFERENCE of the two teams' strengths.
real_world_anchor.secondary: A cyclist on a straight road. Pedal-force and tailwind both push forward — angle zero, they simply add. The same cyclist crossing a bridge with a crosswind — angle ninety, net force tilts and uses Pythagoras. Finally, cycling against a head wind — angle one hundred eighty, the net push is pedal-force minus wind-force.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: Two lifts in a building shaft. Both moving up together — parallel, speeds add. One going up, one going down — antiparallel, the passenger sees the sum of the speeds but in opposite directions. A cable car climbing a hill with wind coming from the side — the two velocities combine by Pythagoras.

formulas.theta_0: theta = 0 deg  =>  R = A + B  (maximum possible)

constraints: All three cases are derived from R = sqrt(A^2 + B^2 + 2 A B cos theta) by substituting cos 0 = 1, cos 90 = 0, cos 180 = -1
At 90 deg, the middle term vanishes — only then Pythagoras applies
Maximum resultant occurs at theta = 0, minimum at theta = 180
The three cases form the boundary markers of the full range |A - B| <= R <= A + B

STATE_1.title: Parallel — theta = 0, Two Kids Pushing a Swing

scene_composition.text:
Two vectors in the SAME direction
(theta = 0 deg, cos 0 = 1)

R^2 = A^2 + B^2 + 2 A B (1)
    = (A + B)^2

=> R = A + B   (maximum)

Scalar addition works — and ONLY
in this special case.

tts_sentences.text_en: Two kids pushing a swing from behind, both pushing in the same direction. The angle between their pushes is zero.
One pushes with four newtons, the other with three. The swing feels the full seven newtons — they simply add.
This is the one and only case where scalar addition gives the correct vector sum. Plug zero into the master formula, cosine zero is one, and the expression collapses to A plus B whole squared.
So when you read that two forces act in the same direction, go ahead and add their magnitudes. You are safe.
For every other angle, you need the full formula. The next two states cover the most exam-relevant ones — ninety degrees and one hundred eighty.

STATE_2.title: Perpendicular — theta = 90, Kite on Makar Sankranti

scene_composition.text:
Perpendicular vectors
(theta = 90 deg, cos 90 = 0)

R^2 = A^2 + B^2 + 2 A B (0)
    = A^2 + B^2

=> R = sqrt(A^2 + B^2)
     = sqrt(16 + 9)
     = 5 N

This is the ONLY case where
pure Pythagoras is correct.

tts_sentences.text_en: Now imagine flying a kite on Makar Sankranti. The wind pushes the kite horizontally with four newtons. The string tugs it upward with three newtons. The two forces are perpendicular — ninety degrees apart.
Plug ninety degrees into the master formula. Cosine ninety is zero, so the entire middle term vanishes.
What remains is pure Pythagoras. R squared equals A squared plus B squared.
Four squared is sixteen, three squared is nine, sum is twenty-five, square root is five.
The kite feels a net pull of five newtons, tilted at thirty-seven degrees above horizontal. That is why the kite sits at that particular tilt in the sky.
Important — Pythagoras only works at ninety degrees. Students apply it to every angle, and it is wrong everywhere else.

scene_composition.text:
Opposite directions
(theta = 180 deg, cos 180 = -1)

R^2 = A^2 + B^2 + 2 A B (-1)
    = A^2 - 2 A B + B^2
    = (A - B)^2

=> R = |A - B|   (minimum)

The larger team wins by the
difference, not by the sum.

tts_sentences.text_en: On a school sports day, two tug-of-war teams grip opposite ends of the same rope.
Team Red pulls with four newtons to the right. Team Blue pulls with three newtons to the left. The forces are exactly opposite — angle one hundred eighty degrees.
Plug one hundred eighty into the formula. Cosine is minus one, so the middle term becomes minus two A B.
The whole expression collapses to A minus B whole squared. Take the square root and use absolute value to stay positive.
Red wins by one newton — that is the difference, not the sum. The rope moves in the direction of the stronger team.
This is also the minimum possible resultant. Whatever the angle, R can never drop below the absolute difference of A and B.

STATE_4.title: All Three on One Page — Memorise This Summary

scene_composition.text: Three angles you must recognise instantly

theta = 0 deg

R = A + B
(maximum)

Examples
- two kids pushing swing
- tailwind on a cyclist
- two lifts moving up together

theta = 90 deg

R = sqrt(A^2 + B^2)
(Pythagoras)

Examples
- kite: wind and string
- boat: paddle and current
- crosswind on a bridge

theta = 180 deg

R = |A - B|
(minimum)

Examples
- tug-of-war
- headwind on a cyclist
- up-lift meeting down-lift

tts_sentences.text_en: These three frames are your mental toolkit for vector-addition problems.
Angle zero — simple addition. Angle ninety — Pythagoras. Angle one hundred eighty — subtraction.
Every other angle needs the full formula with its cosine term. But if a question specifies one of these three angles, you can skip the master formula and use the shortcut directly.

A warning — Pythagoras only applies at exactly ninety degrees. Using it at sixty or at one hundred twenty is a classic exam trap. Stick to the master formula if the angle is anything other than zero, ninety, or one hundred eighty.

epic_c_branches.misconception: R = sqrt(A^2 + B^2) works for any angle, not just 90 degrees
trigger_phrases: R squared is A squared plus B squared
use root of sum of squares

scene_composition.text:
Your formula

R = sqrt(A^2 + B^2)
  = sqrt(16 + 9)
  = 5

You got 5 N.
Let us see if that is right
when the angle is 60, not 90.

tts_sentences.text_en: Students often use Pythagoras for every vector-addition problem. Square both, add, take the root.
At ninety degrees, this is correct. At any other angle, it leaves out a crucial term.
At sixty degrees, you would calculate the square root of twenty-five equals five. Let us check that against the master formula.

STATE_2.title: The Missing Term — Why Pythagoras Breaks

scene_composition.text:
Master formula
R^2 = A^2 + B^2 + 2 A B cos theta

Pythagoras assumes the last term is zero.
That is true ONLY at theta = 90.

At theta = 60, cos 60 = 0.5
2 A B cos theta = 2 * 4 * 3 * 0.5 = 12

Correct
R^2 = 16 + 9 + 12 = 37
R = sqrt(37) ~ 6.08

Pythagoras alone said R = 5.
Gap of over 1 newton.

tts_sentences.text_en: Expand the master formula. R squared equals A squared plus B squared PLUS twice A B times cosine of the angle.
Pythagoras silently assumes the middle term is zero. That is true at ninety degrees, because cosine ninety equals zero.
At sixty degrees, cosine is half. The middle term is twelve. That contribution cannot be ignored.
The correct R squared is thirty-seven, so R is about six point zero eight. Pythagoras alone gave five. You were off by over a newton.
The cosine term is the memory of the angle. Throw it away, and you throw away the angle dependence itself.

STATE_3.title: When You Are Allowed to Use Pythagoras

scene_composition.text:
Pythagoras shortcut

Permitted ONLY when
theta = 90 degrees exactly.

Signs the problem gives you
- 'perpendicular'
- 'at right angles'
- 'horizontal and vertical'
- one vector along x, other along y

If you see any of these -> Pythagoras.
If you see an angle that is NOT 90 ->
use the full formula.

Exam tip

Even when the problem seems simple,
check the angle before using Pythagoras.
Many JEE errors are this exact mistake.
When You Are Allowed to Use Pythagoras

tts_sentences.text_en: Pythagoras is a genuine shortcut — but only at ninety degrees. Recognise the words that tell you the angle is ninety.
Perpendicular. At right angles. One vector horizontal, the other vertical. Any of these phrases means you can use R squared equals A squared plus B squared.
Any other angle — sixty, one twenty, seventy-five — demands the full formula. No exceptions.
Before you write Pythagoras, check the angle. That one check prevents the most common mistake in this chapter.

regeneration_variants.label: Different magnitudes, same three cases
regeneration_variants.physical_world: A = 5 N, B = 12 N. theta = 0 -> R = 17. theta = 90 -> R = 13. theta = 180 -> R = 7.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Maximum at 0, Pythagoras at 90, minimum at 180 — pattern identical

regeneration_variants.label: Equal magnitudes for symmetry
regeneration_variants.physical_world: A = B = 5 N. theta = 0 -> R = 10. theta = 90 -> R = 5 sqrt(2). theta = 120 -> R = 5. theta = 180 -> R = 0.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Equal magnitudes reduce the formula to R = 2A cos(theta/2)
